questions.q: A Riemannian metric $g$ on a smooth manifold $M$ is:
choices: a smooth assignment $p\mapsto g_p$ of a symmetric bilinear form on $T_pM$
a smooth assignment $p\mapsto g_p$ of a symmetric, positive-definite inner product on $T_pM$
a choice of global chart on $M$
questions.explain: A Riemannian metric is a smooth, symmetric, positive-definite inner product on each tangent space. Symmetry alone is not enough; positive-definiteness is what gives lengths and angles.

questions.q: On the round sphere with $ds^2 = d\theta^2 + \sin^2\theta\,d\varphi^2$, compute the squared length of the vector $v = \partial_\varphi$ at the equator $\theta=\pi/2$.
The hyperbolic half-plane metric is $ds^2 = (du^2+dv^2)/v^2$. What is the hyperbolic length of the vertical segment from $(0,1)$ to $(0,e)$?
questions.explain: Parametrize $v(t)=t$ for $t\in[1,e]$; the length is $\int_1^e dt/t = \ln e - \ln 1 = 1$.

questions.q: The Levi-Civita connection is characterized as the unique affine connection that is:
choices: torsion-free and compatible with the metric ($\nabla g = 0$)
compatible with the metric and has constant Christoffel symbols
questions.explain: The fundamental theorem of Riemannian geometry: there is a unique torsion-free connection with $\nabla g = 0$. Flatness is not required — curvature can be nonzero.

questions.q: The geodesic equation in local coordinates reads:
questions.explain: A geodesic satisfies $\nabla_{\dot\gamma}\dot\gamma = 0$, which in coordinates is $\ddot x^k + \Gamma^k_{ij}\dot x^i \dot x^j = 0$.

questions.q: For the Euclidean metric $g_{ij}=\delta_{ij}$ on $\mathbb{R}^n$, how many of the Christoffel symbols $\Gamma^k_{ij}$ are nonzero?
questions.explain: $\Gamma^k_{ij} = \tfrac12 g^{k\ell}(\partial_i g_{j\ell}+\partial_j g_{i\ell}-\partial_\ell g_{ij})$. With $g_{ij}$ constant, every partial vanishes, so all Christoffels are zero.

questions.q: Bonnet–Myers theorem states that a complete Riemannian $n$-manifold with $\operatorname{Ric}\ge (n-1)\kappa g$ (for $\kappa>0$) is:
choices: compact with $\operatorname{diam}(M)\le \pi/\sqrt{\kappa}$ and finite fundamental group
diffeomorphic to $\mathbb{R}^n$ via the exponential map
questions.explain: Uniformly positive Ricci forces geodesics to refocus within distance $\pi/\sqrt{\kappa}$, which makes $M$ compact with bounded diameter and finite $\pi_1$.

questions.q: On the round 2-sphere of radius $1$, the sectional curvature is:
questions.explain: The unit sphere has constant Gaussian (sectional) curvature $K=+1$.

questions.q: For a geodesic triangle on the round unit 2-sphere enclosing area $A$, the Gauss–Bonnet formula gives the angle excess $(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)-\pi = \int K\,dA$. If $K=1$ and $A=\pi/2$, what is $(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)-\pi$?
questions.explain: With $K=1$, the excess equals the area $A=\pi/2 \approx 1.5708$.

questions.q: The Ricci tensor $\operatorname{Ric}(v,v)$ on an $n$-manifold equals:
choices: the sectional curvature of a specific $2$-plane through $v$
$(n-1)$ times the average sectional curvature over $2$-planes containing $v$
the determinant of the Riemann tensor in normal coordinates
questions.explain: Contracting the Riemann tensor yields $\operatorname{Ric}(v,v)=\sum_i \langle R(v,e_i)e_i,v\rangle$ for an orthonormal basis $\{e_i\}$ with $e_1=v/\|v\|$; summing the $(n-1)$ sectional curvatures of planes through $v$ gives exactly $(n-1)$ times their average.

questions.q: On the round $n$-sphere of radius $1$, every sectional curvature equals $1$. What is the scalar curvature $S$ for $n=3$? (Use $S = n(n-1)\,\overline{K}$ with $\overline{K}$ the average sectional curvature.)
An Einstein manifold is a Riemannian manifold satisfying:
questions.explain: Einstein's condition in pure geometry is $\operatorname{Ric}=\lambda g$: the Ricci tensor is proportional to the metric. Spheres, hyperbolic spaces, and complex projective spaces with their symmetric metrics are all Einstein.

questions.q: On an oriented Riemannian $n$-manifold $(M,g)$, the Riemannian volume form in local coordinates is:
questions.explain: The metric-compatible, orientation-compatible top form is $d\mathrm{vol}_g=\sqrt{\det g_{ij}}\,dx^1\wedge\cdots\wedge dx^n$. The factor $\sqrt{\det g}$ is exactly the volume of the parallelepiped spanned by the coordinate vectors in the metric $g$.

questions.q: For the round metric $ds^2 = d\theta^2 + \sin^2\theta\, d\varphi^2$ on $S^2$ with $\theta\in[0,\pi],\,\varphi\in[0,2\pi)$, what is the total surface area $\int_{S^2} d\mathrm{vol}_g$?
The Laplace-Beltrami operator on $(M,g)$ acts on a smooth function $f$ as:
questions.explain: The metric divergence of $\operatorname{grad}_g f = g^{ij}\partial_j f\,\partial_i$ computed against the volume form $\sqrt{\det g}\,dx^1\wedge\cdots\wedge dx^n$ gives exactly $\Delta_g f = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{\det g}}\partial_i(\sqrt{\det g}\,g^{ij}\partial_j f)$. On Euclidean space ($g_{ij}=\delta_{ij}$) this collapses to the usual Laplacian.
